rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order To Seal Documents
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 3] This tentative ruling is entered by Judge Joseph M. Quinn. The Administrator of the Estate of Masami Miyamoto plans to pursue litigation on behalf of the Estate and, by way of a probate petition, she is asking the court to approve a proposed legal services agreement. In connection with that substantive petition, the Administrator and a beneficiary (collectively "Administrator") filed the instant motion asking the court to remove from the public record in the probate proceedings the very subject of the substantive petition-the proposed legal services agreement. The Administrator's motion to seal the agreement is DENIED. A court may order that a record be filed under seal only if it expressly finds facts that establish: (1) an overriding interest exists that overcomes the right of public access to the record; (2) the overriding interest supports sealing the records; (3) a substantial probability exists that the overriding interest will be prejudiced if the record is not sealed; (4) the proposed sealing is narrowly tailored; and, (5) no less restrictive means exist to achieve the overriding interest. (CRC 2.550(d); NBC Subsidiary (KNBC-TV), Inc. v. Sup. Ct. (Locke) (1999) 20 Cal.4th 1178, 1216-1218). The party seeking the sealing of a record bears the burden of presenting information sufficient to identify the specific information claimed to be entitled to such treatment and the nature of the harm threatened by disclosure. (H.B. Fuller Co. v. Doe (2007) 151 Cal.App.4th 879, 894.) The moving party must set forth specific facts in support of a motion to seal; statements that are too conclusory, abstract, or generic are not sufficient to satisfy the burden. (Id. at 895-896.) The rule 2.550 analysis requires the Administrator to make the threshold showing that the public right to access is overridden by the moving party's interest in confidentiality of the particular record. Here, the public right to access is substantial. In probate proceedings broad notice and opportunity to object is fundamental. Notice is not provided when the basic information submitted to support the requested judicial action is withheld from interested parties and the public. Further, public access to the records upon which probate decisions are based furthers judicial accountability. The legal services agreement and its terms are basic to the court's consideration of the Administrator's request to approve the agreement. Any consideration of the Administrator's requests will require discussion of the agreement and its terms. The court's concern goes beyond the pending petition. Looking ahead, when it considers the Administrator's petition(s) to approve extraordinary fees under CRC 7.702 and Local Rule 14.51, as well as the Administrator's accounting petitions and petitions for preliminary or final distribution, the court will have to refer to information in the legal services agreement, such as the identity and experience of the attorney(s) representing the Administrator, the scope of the engagement, the attorney and support staff's hourly rates. These basic facts drawn from the agreement will likely be the topic of discussion and will necessarily inform the court's resolution of these and other petitions. [Part 1 of 3]

rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion For Order To Seal Documents (Added For Posting Of The Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 3] The Administrator has a confidentiality interest in the agreement. The agreement is privileged against compelled disclosure under Evidence Code section 952. But, of course, the Administrator holds the privilege. She need not pursue judicial approval of the legal services agreement; but, if she chooses to do so and asks the court to consider-indeed rely on-the agreement to grant substantive relief, then she will have to make the agreement available so that the court can fulfill its duty to act only after broad notice and opportunity to object. There is no general right to confidentiality of a legal services agreement in this specific context. Instructive on this point are Probate Code section 10811(c), which requires filing a proposed contingent fee agreement with the probate court for approval, and related case law, which holds that the agreement need not to be sealed (see Estate of Stevenson (2006) 141 Cal. App. 4th 1074, 1087-88). Nothing about the legal services agreement at issue in this case excepts it from this general rule. This case does not involve trade secrets nor does the record suggest that disclosure will necessarily harm the Estate's financial interests. (See McGuan v. Endovascular Techs., Inc. (2010) 182 Cal.App.4th 974, 988.) The court finds in this case that the public right to access predominates. Even if the Administrator had shown that her interest in confidentiality of the agreement predominated, she has not shown specific facts establishing a substantial probability that her interest in confidentiality will be prejudiced if the agreement is not sealed. The Administrator's argument that the Estate would be prejudiced in the civil action if the agreement were filed on the public record is not supported by any facts or specifics. The argument that a probate court must always seal a fee agreement lest it reveal litigation strategy has already been rejected in Estate of Stevenson (2006) 141 Cal. App. 4th 1074, 1087: "Further, litigants are not permitted to play 'hide and seek' with respect to theories of liability. The estate had no choice but to disclose its so-called litigation strategy in various papers filed with the court during the course of the litigation-in its petition to establish ownership of property, reply to the creditor's objections, mediation brief, and trial brief. There can be little reason to keep something secret when routine court proceedings require that it be disclosed before and after [a] hearing." And, here, the legal services agreement is quite standard and does not detail anything specific about the civil litigation or the estate administration. Nor has the Administrator shown that the proposed sealing is narrowly tailored or that it is the least restrictive means to protect her confidentiality interest. The proposed sealing, in fact, is not at all tailored; instead, the Administrator seeks to shelter from public view the entire agreement. Surely, the Administrator cannot reasonably expect that new counsel's identity would be kept secret or even that their hourly rates would not be subject to disclosure in this proceeding. Even if the Administrator could point to sensitive provisions, legal opinions or analysis or strategic information in the agreement, sealing wouldn't be the remedy. The best-tailored remedy in that case would be to revise the agreement to eliminate the sensitive information. A legal services agreement need not contain anything other than standard provisions and basic information. Such basic information (and a public discussion of the information) is necessary to the court's adjudication of the Administrator's request for an order approving retention of new counsel and payment of extraordinary fees to counsel, as well as other requests the Administrator will surely present to the court. [Part 2 of 3]

rulings.Rulings: [Part 3 of 3] Petitioner shall prepare and lodge with the court clerk in Department 204 a hard-copy proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling prior to the time set for the hearing. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on April 22, 2021. Remote appearance is strongly encouraged, in conformity with the current General Order of the Probate and Mental Health Division, available on the court's website at https://sfsuperiorcourt.org/divisions/probate. To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 87580177925 and passcode 722809. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 87580177925#, participant ID 0#, and passcode 722809#. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204. [Part 3 of 3]
